title: Revert "[InstCombine] Infer nuw for gep inbounds from base of object"

tests.source_program:
define i32 @test_alloca_store_load(i32 %idx, i32 %val) {
  %alloca = alloca [10 x i32], align 4
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds [10 x i32], ptr %alloca, i32 0, i32 %idx
  store i32 %val, ptr %gep, align 4
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
define i32 @test_alloca_store_load(i32 %idx, i32 %val) {
  %alloca = alloca [10 x i32], align 4
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds nuw [10 x i32], ptr %alloca, i32 0, i32 %idx
  store i32 %val, ptr %gep, align 4
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load
}
tests.outside_scope_reason: GEP used in store operation then load, no memcpy involved - summary focuses on memcpy pattern specifically

tests.source_program:
target datalayout = "e-p:64:64-i64:64-n32:64-S128"

define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %idx, -1
  br i1 %cmp, label %valid, label %invalid

valid:
  %alloca = alloca [100 x i32], align 4
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds [100 x i32], ptr %alloca, i32 0, i32 %idx
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load

invalid:
  ret i32 0
}
tests.expect_optimized_program:
target datalayout = "e-p:64:64-i64:64-n32:64-S128"

define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %idx, -1
  br i1 %cmp, label %valid, label %invalid

valid:
  %alloca = alloca [100 x i32], align 4
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds nuw [100 x i32], ptr %alloca, i32 0, i32 %idx
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load

invalid:
  ret i32 0
}
tests.outside_scope_reason: Known tests rely on memcpy to establish object bounds. This test proves index non-negativity through icmp control flow instead, showing the optimization works with nneg analysis from any source, not just memcpy-derived patterns.

target datalayout = "e-p:64:64-i64:64-n32:64-S128"

define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %idx, -1
  br i1 %cmp, label %valid, label %invalid

valid:
  %alloca = alloca [100 x i32], align 4
  %gep = getelementptr inbounds [100 x i32], ptr %alloca, i32 0, i32 %idx
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load

invalid:
  ret i32 0
}
alive2.tgt:
; ModuleID = '<stdin>'
source_filename = "<stdin>"
target datalayout = "e-p:64:64-i64:64-n32:64-S128"

define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
  br i1 false, label %valid, label %invalid

valid:                                            ; preds = %0
  ret i32 poison

invalid:                                          ; preds = %0
  ret i32 0
}
alive2.log:
----------------------------------------
define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
#0:
  %cmp = icmp ugt i32 %idx, 4294967295
  br i1 %cmp, label %valid, label %invalid

invalid:
  ret i32 0

valid:
  %alloca = alloca i64 400, align 4
  %gep = gep inbounds ptr %alloca, 400 x i32 0, 4 x i32 %idx
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep, align 4
  ret i32 %load
}
=>
define i32 @nneg_index_via_icmp_no_memcpy(i32 %idx) {
#0:
  br i1 0, label %valid, label %invalid

invalid:
  ret i32 0

valid:
  ret i32 poison
}

tests.outside_scope_reason: Chain of GEPs through nested struct types with multiple field indices. The summary focuses on simple array GEPs from alloca/memcpy patterns, not complex struct navigation with multiple inbounds GEPs in sequence.

alive2.log:
----------------------------------------
@struct_arr = constant 192 bytes, align 16

define i32 @gep_struct_with_multiple_field_indices(i64 %outer_idx, i64 %inner_idx) {
#0:
  %gep1 = gep inbounds ptr @struct_arr, 192 x i64 %outer_idx
  %gep2 = gep inbounds ptr %gep1, 48 x i64 0, 1 x i64 0
  %gep3 = gep inbounds ptr %gep2, 48 x i64 0, 12 x i64 %inner_idx
  %gep4 = gep inbounds ptr %gep3, 12 x i64 0, 1 x i64 4
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep4, align 4
  ret i32 %load
}
=>
@struct_arr = constant 192 bytes, align 16

define i32 @gep_struct_with_multiple_field_indices(i64 %outer_idx, i64 %inner_idx) {
#0:
  %gep1 = gep inbounds ptr @struct_arr, 192 x i64 %outer_idx
  %gep4 = gep inbounds ptr %gep1, 48 x i64 0, 12 x i64 %inner_idx, 1 x i64 4
  %load = load i32, ptr %gep4, align 4
  ret i32 %load
}
Transformation seems to be correct!
